courses.description:
(UGEB1870 is double-coded with EESC1001.)
The course provides an interdisciplinary study of the Earth Systems, including natural phenomena in the atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, cryosphere, and biosphere. The mechanism of these systems, associated dynamics and anthropogenic impacts will be introduced and discussed. The course also serves as a platform to understand and critically assess global environmental change, natural hazards, biodiversity loss, and the water-energy-food nexus, and explore potential technological and policy solutions to these problems. These objectives align strongly with the understanding and pursuit of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, especially SDG#7 Affordable and Clean Energy, SDG#13 Climate Action, SDG#14 Life Below Water, and SDG#15 Life on Land. In addition to attending lectures, students will visit the Jockey Club Museum of Climate Change where it displays the way scientists examine past and current climate and the factors leading to current global temperature change. A geological field excursion will be another highlight of the course to demonstrate the scientific concepts applied to reveal Earth’s tectonic history, evolution and interaction among various spheres. With diverse scientific topics in Earth’s systems, the course shows students how each Earth’s system is interlocking with other spheres and, hence raise the awareness in the importance of sustainability.

Experiential Learning Course
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Describe and explain how the atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, cryosphere, and biosphere interact and how feedback mechanisms operate among the Earth systems.
2. Develop an interdisciplinary approach for investigating Earth system dynamics.
3. Discuss the anthropogenic influences, as well as scientific and technical challenges related to global environmental changes, natural hazards, and the food-water-energy nexus critically.
4. Recognize the complexity of linkages between environmental pressures and human responses related to contemporary sustainable development challenges, contributing to the understanding and pursuit of SDG#7 Affordable and Clean Energy, SDG#13 Climate Action, SDG#14 Life below Water, and SDG#15 Life on Land.
5. Communicate effectively about Earth system and environmental issues in an appropriate science format (i.e. scientific poster and QnA) through working independently and collaboratively.
courses.course_syllabus:
| * ***Lectures***: To provide students basic concepts of different spheres, background information about present environmental phenomena and scientific approaches to study the Earth system * ***Tutorial:*** To guide students critically think about Earth system and related issues to our environment. * ***Visit:*** To show students how professional field works to understand our Earth * ***Field trip:*** To facilitate students observe the environment and interpret and discuss related environmental problems *Note:* Students are expected to attend all lectures, and participate in all tutorials, the visit and the field trip. Meeting with academic advisors can be arranged upon request. **Course Syllabus (English) (Tentative)** The course content will be divided into three parts: **Part I: Geosphere and Cryosphere (Week 1-5)** This component focuses on studying the solid Earth using gravity, electromagnetic and seismic methods. Students will be introduced to the solid physical foundations of the solid Earth, as well as an overview of the structure and composition of the Earth, its materials and constituents, dynamics of its internal and external processes. **Part II: Atmosphere and Climate (Week 7-9)** This compenent covers an integrated introduction to the climate system and the dynamics and chemistry of the atmosphere. Students will learn to explain the history, current state and future projection of weather and climate, natural hazards, and global climate change in the context of natural variability and anthropogenic influence. **Part III: Ecosystem Interactions (Week 10-12)** This compenent covers the interactions of the terrestrial and marine biosphere with the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere. courses.description: This course presents an integrated introduction to the solid earth system stressing the geological, chemical, physical, and biological interactions among the lithosphere, hydrosphere and cryosphere. The course applies basic scientific and mathematical concepts to explain the history and behaviour of the solid earth system. Geophysical and geochemical techniques for probing the interior of the earth are reviewed. Plate tectonics is introduced as a unifying theme to understand geodynamic processes. The course includes discussions of topics that are relevant to society, resources and sustainability, such as the physical and chemical basis of natural hazards (earthquakes, tsunami, landslides) and their mitigation, and the impact of geological processes on the global environment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC2010.
courses.learning_outcomes: This course will contribute to the understanding of the dynamics of the solid Earth system, specifically the lithosphere, hydrosphere and cryosphere, as well as their interaction. The students should be able to: 1) describe and use the scientific process, explain how theories are built upon earth system observations, and how both are tested and revised; 2) explain how fundamental physical, chemical, and biological principles govern specific earth system processes; 3) participate knowledgeably and intelligently in discussions of public policy related to earth system science, particularly with respect to natural hazards, resources, environment and sustainable development; 4) work effectively in a team, and present information in a clear, concise and logical manner.

courses.description: This course presents an integrated introduction to the climate system, stressing the dynamics of the atmosphere and its physical and chemical interactions with the hydrosphere, biosphere and geosphere. The course applies basic scientific and mathematical principles to explain the history, current state and future projection of weather and climate, natural hazards (e.g., typhoons, floods), and global climate change in the context of natural variability and anthropogenic influence. Topics include Earth’s energy balance, climate feedback, convection and clouds, general circulation of the atmosphere and ocean, biogeochemistry and global carbon cycle, roles of vegetation and ecosystems, and historical and future climate change. Student taking this course are expected to have taken PHYS1111, CHEM1070 and MATH1010 or equivalent.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC2020.
courses.learning_outcomes: This course will contribute to the understanding of the dynamics of the climate system, specifically the atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere and solid earth, as well as their interactions. The students should be able to: 1) describe and use the scientific processes, explain how theories are built upon earth system observations, and how both are tested and revised; 2) explain how fundamental physical and chemical principles govern specific climate system processes; 3) participate knowledgeably and intelligently in discussions of public policy related to earth system science, particularly with respect to extreme weather events, global climate change and sustainable development; 4) work effectively in a team, and present information in a clear, concise and logical manner.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Topics**

Introduction; overview of the climate system

* *Part** **I: Energy Balance of the Earth**

Basic radiation physics; energy balance of the Earth; greenhouse effect

Simple climate model; radiative forcing, climate sensitivity and climate feedback

* *Lab 1:** Climate sensitivity and feedback in a simple climate model

Climate variability and climate change; roles of aerosols; meridional structure of the atmosphere

* *Part** **II: Dynamics of the Atmosphere**

Vertical structure of the atmosphere; Newton’s second law; pressure and hydrostatic balance; barometric law

Convection; first law of thermodynamics; dry adiabatic lapse rate; atmospheric stability

Potential temperature; water content in air; moist convection and cloud formation

* *Lab 2:** Conservation of angular momentum on a rotating Earth

Coriolis force; pressure gradient force; horizontal momentum equations; geostrophic balance

Thermal wind balance; atmospheric fronts and jet stream; surface friction and ageostrophic flow

General circulation of the atmosphere; Hadley circulation in the tropics; baroclinic eddies and midlatitude weather systems

* *Lab 3:** Introduction to weather analysis and forecast

* *Field** **trip to** **Hong Kong Observatory**

* *Part** **III: Ocean and Climate**

Overview of ocean circulation; Ekman transport and wind-driven circulation

Effect of Ekman transport on the interior ocean; western boundary currents; thermohaline circulation

Ocean heat buffer and transport; El Niño-Southern Oscillation; tropical cyclones

* *Part** **IV:** **Ecosystems** **and Climate**

Fluxes in biosphere-atmosphere interactions; surface energy balance; evapotranspiration and photosynthesis; biogeophysical feedbacks

Biogeochemical reservoirs and residence times; carbon and nutrient cycling in ecosystems; geological carbon cycle

Contemporary carbon cycle; marine carbonate chemistry; biological bump; ocean biogeochemical feedbacks

* *Lab 4**: Global carbon cycle

Global carbon budget; terrestrial carbon sink and biogeochemical feedbacks

* *Part** **V:** **Past,** **Present** **and Future Climate**

Climate throughout Earth’s history; atmosphere of the early Earth; from Cretaceous to ice ages; recent 20,000 years

Recent climate change: observations and attribution

Future climate projections; impacts of climate change; where do we go from here?

courses.description: This course covers basic computation skills which are important to modern Earth and Environmental Sciences research and data analysis. Topics include the basic Python language syntax; programming flow control; usage of functions; file input/output modules; data visualisation; atmospheric science applications; geoscience applications and basic machine learning applications in earth and environmental sciences. Python 3 will be used throughout the course. Examples and tutorials will be based on various problems in Earth and Environmental Sciences using actual observational or in-situ data.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken CSCI1040, CSCI2040 or ESSC2030.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will obtain basic skills in coding for the tasks in different earth science problems. This includes data acquisition, selection and manipulation in geophysical and environmental problems. They will also learn specific Python modules in science applications.
courses.course_syllabus:

* Methods, Functions, and Numpy
* Booleans, If-then-else Statement
* For Loop and While Loop, Try-except
* Self-defined Functions, Programming Style
* File IO with Pandas and Xarray
* Data Visualization with Matplotlib
* Scientific Computing with Scipy

courses.description: EESC2160 is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of minerals as fundamental building blocks of the Earth, their chemistry, occurrence, physical and chemical features, crystallography, as well as the formation and uses of naturally formed minerals, oil, and gas. The course also covers mining processes and management, exploring the interaction between natural resources and the environment, and their impact on the biological system. The course teaches students chemistry in the Solid Earth, occurrence, exploration and usage of natural resources like minerals, gas and oil. The course trains students with skills of identifying physical properties and chemical properties of natural forming minerals. Teachers will introduce techniques and technology in mining and extracting natural resources. Areas of interaction between natural resources and the environment, as well as the management of natural resources, will be elaborated in student’s problem-based learning sessions. Students are expected to equip knowledge and skills in understanding the real-world scenarios of the natural resources, and problem-solving skills in resource management practices and decision-making processes. These objectives align strongly with the understanding and pursuit of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, especially SDG#7 Affordable and Clean Energy, SDG#11 Raise the public awareness in urgent actions to combat climate change, and SDG#12 Promote responsibility in global environmental changes.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students will be able to:

* Describe the rock-forming minerals in terms of their chemistry, structural characteristics, and physical properties.
* Discuss the relationship between the chemical composition, structural features, and physical properties of minerals.
* Describe the mineral assemblages commonly found in igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.
* Understand the formation processes and practical applications of natural resources, including minerals, oil, and gas.
* Gain knowledge of mining processes and management techniques, including environmental considerations.
* Analyze the interaction between natural resources and the environment, as well as their impact on biological systems.
* Develop problem-solving skills through the application of natural resource management principles.
* Recognize the complexity and linkages among the natural resource exploration, associated impacts and environmental management.
* Evaluate the needs of the present-day society and sustainable development challenges, contributing to the understanding and pursuit SDG#7 Affordable and Clean Energy, SDG#11 Raise the public awareness in urgent actions to combat climate change, and SDG#12 Promote responsibility in global environmental changes.
courses.course_syllabus:
Course Content:

1. Chemistry in the Solid Earth

2. Minerals Formation and Exploration

3. Physical and Mineral Properties of Naturally Formed Minerals

4. Uses of Naturally Occurring Minerals

5. Oil and Gas Formation and Exploration

6. Interaction between Natural Resources and the Environment

7. Interaction between Natural Resources and Biological Systems

8. Problem-Based Learning of Natural Resources and Management

Course Activities:

Classroom teaching: introduce the key topics

Laboratory practical sessions: provide hands-on practice to study the prominent features of common minerals, ore minerals and gemstones

Field study: physically visit a mining site as a case study to understand the science and operation of extracting valuable natural resources and the challenges

Problem-based learning: students are conducting research, discussion and presentation to a given issue of natural resources

Final Exam: Students take final exam to assess their understanding of the course contents

courses.description: This course deals with the relationships between living organisms, resources and environment. The basic multidisciplinary concepts of sustainable development, human population and health risk, conservation of resources, climate change, environmental degradation, pollution control, biodiversity, solid waste and waste water management, etc, will be introduced. Concepts of think globally, act locally will be introduced the importance of acting as a global citizen to help solving environmental problems with an innovative mind to design new policy or technology. Case studies and field trips are included to illustrate various environmental issues using a multidisciplinary approach with emphasis on how modern technology can have significant societal impacts and responsibility.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Environmental Chemistry
courses.description: This is a foundation course designed to equip students with a proper understanding of the fundamental aspects of environmental chemistry. It introduces the basic concept of chemical equilibrium and kinetics, the nature and characteristics of various common types of air and water pollution arising from industrial, agricultural and domestic sources. It outlines the principles of measurement for various parameters commonly used for air and water pollution studies, their significance and limitations. It also examines various types of aerial emission arising from combustion and industrial processes, and vehicles, and their chemical interaction leading to various global environmental issues such as the formation of photochemical smog, depletion of ozone layer and global warming effects.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ENSC2515. Pre-requisite: EESC2270 or ENSC2270 or permission of the instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students who have finished this course are expected to understand:

1. some basic concepts in chemistry ranging from atomic structure and chemical bonding to the basic principle of coordination chemistry of the transition metals, and thermodynamics and kinetics.
2. aquatic chemistry which includes solubility, acid-base reactions, complexation equilibria, and redox reactions.
3. the chemistry of common pollutants in natural water systems.
4. atmospheric chemistry with emphasis on chemical and photochemical processes occurring in the atmosphere.
5. the chemistry of common air pollutants.
6. the chemistry of soil including its composition, essential nutrients in soil, the chemistry of fertilizers, and acid-base and ion-exchange reactions in soil.

Student will understand key ideas on these topics and understand various local and global environmental problems through a case-study approach.

courses.description: This is a foundation chemistry laboratory course designed to accompany EESC2515. This course illustrates the fundamental principles and basic techniques in general environmental chemistry. Laboratory work covers a range of experiments, including acid-base reactions, chemical thermodynamics and kinetics, complexation equilibria, coordination chemistry of the transition metals, and analysis of air pollutants. Students who finish this course are expected to be familiar with the general techniques in conducting chemistry experiments and the use of simple instruments such as IR and UV–Vis spectrophotometers. They will also learn how to design of experiments and report experimental data in scientific reports. courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering
courses.description: This is a multidisciplinary course introducing the fundamental physical, chemical and biological principles necessary for understanding the natural and built environments, with an emphasis on reliable water and food supplies, pollution mitigation, waste management, energy resources, climate change, and sustainable development. Examples of engineering solutions to tackle some of these resource and environmental issues are presented. Field trips are included to illustrate interesting case studies. Topics include: conservation principles for mass, energy and momentum in the environmental systems; basic environmental chemistry, hydrology and ecology; risk assessment; and basic principles of water resource engineering, solid waste treatment, noise pollution, air pollution control, climate change adaptation and mitigation, and the interplay between energy consumption and the environment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC2800. Pre-requisite: MATH1010 or 1510 or 1520.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Understand the basic physical, chemical and biological principles governing the various resource and environmental issues of our society.
* Be able to use basic mathematical methods to assess environmental risks and the feasibility of various engineering solutions to resource and environmental problems.
* Be able to participate knowledgeably and intelligently in discussion of public policy related to global and regional resource and environmental issues including the food-water-energy crisis, water and air pollution, global climate change, and hazardous wastes.
* Be able to work effectively in a team, and present information in a clear, concise and logical manner.

courses.description: This course is an important part of the core Earth and Environmental Sciences Curriculum. It introduces the processes and products of rock deformation in three dimensions, ranging from micro- (micron) to macro- (kilometer) scales. It discusses geometries and mechanism of faults, folds, structural features of igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic rocks, and superposed deformations and introduces regional deformational history and tectonics. Structural geology in the field of resource exploration (petroleum and mineral deposits) is also introduced. Class lectures are supplemented by lab exercise and demonstrations as well as field trips to local or overseas outcrops.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC3100. Pre-requisite: EESC2010 or ESSC2010 or permission of instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to gain the following abilities:
1. Understand the geometry of different geological structures
2. Use concepts of solid mechanics to interpret the kinematics and geometries of geological structure formation
3. Use graphical tools to compile and interpret structural data
4. Integrate topics in structural geology and plate tectonics through survey of scientific literatures
5. Make primary observations in the field and using them to construct and test structural hypotheses

Lectures focus on geometric, kinematic, and mechanical analysis of geological structures. Labs use geological maps, computers, stereonets, and physical analogs to illustrate these concepts. Students are encouraged to lead discussion of leading geological topics spread throughout the semester, each of which integrates recent class topics with large-scale active tectonics. Field trips in the second half of the semester encourage small-group discussion of observations and interpretations of deformational history of the field working area.

The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Physics of the Earth
courses.description: This course focuses on the concepts of the physics of the Earth to understand how the solid Earth works. Since 1960s, plate tectonics has been developed to explain observations on Earth such as mountain building, occurrence of earthquakes and volcanoes, as well as deposition of natural resources. The discovery of plate tectonics requires understanding of geophysical methods, which will be covered in this course. The topics covered include the Earth’s gravitational and magnetic fields, earthquake seismology, internal structure of the Earth, thermal and electrical properties, as well as geodynamics. Methods of geophysical survey will also be introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC3120. Pre-requisite: EESC 2010 or ESSC2010 or permission of instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes: At the completion of this course, the students will be able to know about Earth’s inner structure, composition, and thermal and electrical properties, understand principles of seismic wave propagation and earthquake physics, gain knowledge of gravity and geomagnetism, and have the ability to analyze the observation data for explanation of geophysical processes. The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.

Plate tectonics: Magnetic field, Paleomagnetism, Plate motion and rotation

Seismology: Earthquakes and plate boundaries, Earthquake location and magnitude, Earthquake energy and

focal mechanism, Wave propagation, Refraction and reflection seismology

Gravity: Gravity and geoid, Gravity corrections, Gravity anomaly, Isostasy

Heat: Heat flow, Heat conduction, Heat convection

courses.description: EESC3140 is designed to introduce students to the global distribution of elements and the chemistry of natural Earth's materials. The course focuses on understanding the chemical processes operating within the solid Earth and other planets, spanning from the past to the present. Emphasis is placed on developing quantitative and analytical skills in geochemistry. The course covers a wide range of topics, including fundamental principles of geochemical processes, thermal dynamics, phase transitions and the relationship between pressure, temperature, and composition in different geological materials, major elements, trace elements, rare Earth elements, element mobility and mineral stability, and the roles of different elements in Earth’s evolution. The course also introduces the role of minerals and rocks in supporting and influencing life and, how biological processes affect the chemical composition of Earth’s atmosphere, hydrosphere, and lithosphere. Students will be trained to acquire a comprehensive understanding of the formation and interaction of materials within the Earth. Students will learn about the geochemical processes involved and their implications for potential tectonic settings and surficial processes. By studying geochemical data and engaging in practical sessions, students will develop the skills to analyze and interpret the geochemical data, allowing them to discuss paleo-environments with possible tectonic models such as seafloor spreading, subduction and mountain building regimes.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: (EESC2010 or ESSC2010) or (EESC2130 or ESSC2130 or EESC2119 or EESC2129) or (EESC3100 or ESSC3100) or permission of instructor
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to gain the following abilities:

1. Understanding the chemical processes operating within the solid Earth.
2. Applying geochemical concepts and techniques to real-world scenarios
3. Discussing and interpreting paleo environments using possible tectonic models, fostering critical thinking and problem-solving skills
4. Summarizing information in a clear, concise and logical manner in the assignments and exams to understand geologic processes, paleo-environments and present geomorphology
5. Working independently and self-study
6. Working in a team and discuss with teammates.
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics cover:

* Introduction to geochemistry (1.5)
* State of matters (2.5)
* Major and minor elements (2)
* Trace elements (2)
* Isotope geochemistry (1)
* Modelling of Earth processes (1)
* Phase equilibrium studies and processes of crystallisation, melting, and metamorphic reactions (4)
* Interactions among the lithosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere, and atmosphere (2)
* Water-rock interaction & carbonate geochemistry (2)
* Weathering and mineral reactions (2)
* Biogeochemical cycles (1)
* Biosphere (1)

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Atmospheric Dynamics
courses.description: This course explores the fundamental dynamical processes that govern atmospheric motion, with a focus on the physical mechanisms driving weather systems and large-scale circulation. Building on foundational fluid dynamics, students will develop a deeper understanding of the equations of motion and their applications to atmospheric phenomena.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1) Pre-requisite: (EESC2020 or ESSC2020) or (MATH2550 or EESC2520 or PHYS2051) or permission of instructor.
2) Not for students who have taken ESSC3200.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of this course, the students will be able to

* Recall and explain the fundamental equations and physical principles governing atmospheric motion.
* Apply key dynamical models, such as the shallow water and quasi-geostrophic equations, to analyze atmospheric flows.
* Analyze wave phenomena and instabilities, including Rossby waves, gravity waves, and baroclinic instability.
* Evaluate the interactions between waves and mean flows, and assess their role in shaping atmospheric circulation.
* Interpret and communicate insights from weather-in-a-tank experiments to illustrate complex atmospheric dynamics.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Fundamentals Refresher – Review of basic fluid dynamics equations, Cartesian approximation on a tangent plane, and the Boussinesq approximation for small density variations.
2. Shallow Water Equations – Derivation and applications in modeling large-scale atmospheric flows.
3. Quasi-Geostrophic Motion – Balance approximations and their role in midlatitude dynamics.
4. Rossby Waves – Planetary wave dynamics and their implications for weather patterns.
5. Gravity Waves – Generation and propagation.
6. Barotropic and Baroclinic Instability – Mechanisms of atmospheric instability and storm development.
7. Wave-Mean-Flow Interactions – Feedbacks between waves and background flow.
8. Vorticity Dynamics – Absolute, relative, and potential vorticity in atmospheric motion.
9. Tropical Circulation – Dynamics of the Hadley cell and its thermodynamic and hydrodynamic constraints.
10. Extratropical Circulation – Dynamics of Ferrel cell and jet streams.
11. Wave Diagnostics – Analyzing wave source, waveguide, and wave propagation.
12. Wave Activity and Blocking – Understanding persistent weather pattern dynamics using wave activity.

courses.description: This course aims to provide students with a basic understanding of the operational principles and applications of various instrumental techniques commonly used for the studies of air and water pollution. It is designed to cover atomic and molecular absorption and emission spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, and electrochemical methods. Emphasis will be placed on the application of such techniques for pollution monitoring and preventive detection systems, especially with reference to the identification and determination of trace levels of toxic heavy metals, organic and organometallic compounds.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Environmental Microbiology

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G01

courses.description: Environmental Microbiology is an interdisciplinary course that explores the fascinating world of microorganisms and their impact on the environment. This comprehensive course covers both the scientific fundamentals and practical applications in the field of Environmental Microbiology. This course will begin with an introduction to the field by covering topics such as the formation of the biosphere, and the science of cell biology. Then the course goes beyond theory and delves into the methods used in environmental microbiology to study microorganisms in their natural habitats. They will also examine the applications of environmental microbiology, such as studying the role of bacteria in climate change, biodegradation, and bioremediation. These practical applications highlight how microorganisms can be harnessed to address environmental challenges and contribute to a sustainable future.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: LSCI1002 or LSCI1012 or EESC2270, or permission of the instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students would be able to:

1. Demonstrate knowledge of the formation of the biosphere and the role of microorganisms in shaping ecosystems.
2. Describe the basic cellular structures, metabolic processes, and adaptation strategies of microorganisms in various environments in scientific terms.
3. Recognize appropriate methods and techniques for studying microorganisms in their natural habitats, including sample collection, isolation, and community analysis.
4. Evaluate the application of biodegradation, bioremediation technologies and microbial consortia in environmental cleanup and restoration.
5. Apply knowledge and practical insights from environmental microbiology to address real-world environmental challenges and contribute to sustainability efforts.
courses.course_syllabus:
* Introduction to Environmental Microbiology
* Formation of the Biosphere
* Microorganisms: Cell Biology
* Microorganisms: Nutrition and Growth
* Microorganisms: Microbial Diversity
* Methods in Environmental Microbiology
* Microbial Habitats: Soil and Water
* Microbial Habitats: Life in extreme environments
* Application: Bacteria and climate change
* Application: Biodegradation
* Application: Bioremediation

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Quantitative Methods in Environmental Management
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive exploration of environmental management strategies aimed at mitigating the impact of environmental hazards on ecosystems and human health. Emphasizing quantitative methods, the curriculum covers environmental regulations, ethical considerations, monitoring techniques, ecological risk assessment, climate change impacts, sustainable resource management, and the application of quantitative tools such as decision support systems, ecometrics, and stochastic empirical loading models. Through case studies and practical applications, students will develop the skills necessary to design and implement effective environmental management plans that promote sustainability and protect natural resources.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ENSC3520. Pre-requisite: EESC2270 or permission of the instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. **Assess the Impact of Environmental Hazards**: Evaluate the effects of environmental hazards on human health, wildlife, and ecosystems, considering factors such as toxicity, exposure pathways, and cumulative impacts.
2. **Apply Quantitative Decision Tools:** Utilize quantitative decision-making tools to analyze complex environmental issues, evaluate alternative solutions, and make informed, data-driven decisions that promote sustainable environmental management practices.
3. **Develop and Implement Environmental Management Strategies**: Design and apply effective approaches for pollution prevention, remediation, and sustainable resource management to mitigate environmental contamination.
4. **Conduct Environmental Risk Assessments**: Identify potential environmental hazards, assess associated risks, and prioritize actions to protect environmental and public health.
5. **Understand Regulatory Frameworks and Compliance**: Interpret and apply environmental laws, regulations, and standards to ensure compliance and promote environmental stewardship.
6. **Communicate Environmental Information Effectively**: Present complex environmental data and management plans clearly to diverse audiences, facilitating informed decision-making and stakeholder engagement.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Environmental Management and Chemical Contaminants
2. Environmental Regulations and Policies
3. Environmental Ethics and Social Responsibility
4. Environmental Monitoring and Data Analysis
5. Ecological Risk Assessment
6. Climate Change and Environmental Management
7. Sustainable Resource Management
8. Application of Quantitative Tools in Environmental Management
9. Environmental Communication and Advocacy
10. Case Studies in Environmental Management
11. Future Directions in Environmental Management

courses.title: Environmental Quality Monitoring and Analysis
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles and practices of environmental quality monitoring and analysis. Students will develop a solid understanding of the key parameters that affect air, water, and soil quality, learn various monitoring and sampling techniques, and analyze environmental quality conditions and trends. The course will cover theoretical concepts as well as practical application to real-world environmental management scenarios.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: (CHEM1070 or CHEM1072) or (EESC2515 or ENSC2515) or permission of the instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

1. Identify and describe the major environmental parameters and pollutants that impact environmental quality.
2. Explain the regulatory frameworks and standards related to environmental quality monitoring and compliance.
3. Demonstrate proficiency in sampling techniques and laboratory analysis methods for environmental quality monitoring.
4. Analyze and interpret environmental quality data using statistical and graphical tools.
5. Evaluate the effectiveness of environmental management strategies based on monitoring and analysis results.
courses.course_syllabus:
Module 1: Introduction to Environmental Quality Monitoring

* Overview of environmental quality monitoring and its significance in environmental management
* Introduction to key environmental parameters and pollutants

Module 2: Air Quality Monitoring and Analysis

* Sampling techniques and analysis methods for air quality monitoring
* Interpretation of air quality data and identification of pollutants

Module 3: Water Quality Monitoring and Analysis

* Sampling techniques and analysis methods for water quality monitoring
* Evaluation of water quality parameters and their impact on ecosystems and human health

Module 4: Soil Quality Monitoring and Analysis

* Sampling techniques and analysis methods for soil quality monitoring
* Assessment of soil contamination and its implications for environmental sustainability

Module 5: Environmental Quality Data Visualization and Analysis (Using R)

* Statistical analysis of water quality data
* Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
* Regression analysis
* Trend analysis and Analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA)
* Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
* Interpretation of data for decision-making

Module 6: Environmental Management and Compliance

* Evaluation of environmental management strategies based on monitoring and analysis results
* Understanding regulatory frameworks and standards for environmental quality monitoring

courses.description: This course introduces the physical, chemical and biological principles governing the functioning of terrestrial and marine ecosystems, and their roles in shaping the Earth’s climatic and geological environments. Topics include: basic hydrology and surface energy balance; energy, momentum, water and chemical exchange between ecosystems and the atmosphere; cycling of water, carbon and other nutrients in soil, vegetation and ocean; survival strategies of different life forms (especially plants and microorganisms); ecosystem structures and functions; landscapes, disturbances and ecosystem dynamics; human perturbations of biogeochemical cycles via pollution, greenhouse gas emissions and land use; responses and evolution of terrestrial and marine ecosystems under global climate change. This course serves as a comprehensive introduction to ecological climatology, biogeochemistry and ecosystem ecology, and requires basic mathematical operations. This version of the course includes an integrated set of computer laboratory sessions.

* Topic 5: Global carbon cycle and biogeochemical feedbacks

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Global Environmental Change
courses.description:
This course includes the science behind the global warming issue, how the public views the issue and the critical thinking needed to analyze the evidence seen in the media/articles from the point of view of scientists. The world energy use, which is considered to be the cause of global warming, and the impacts are discussed in the course. In this course, students will learn about the radiation budgets/balance in the atmosphere, possible climate forcings that can upset the energy balance, how the earth’s global average temperature is measured, why global warming is described as “the biggest hoax” in human history, observations from the ocean and cryosphere and popular environmental issues/arguments discussed in the mainstream media. Newest observations from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) report are included throughout the course.

This course actively incorporates elements of innovation, design, global citizenship and social enterprisingness by asking the students to think innovatively but critically about possible solutions to adapt to and combat climate change and the global energy/health crisis. Different options including their pros and cons are actively discussed in class. Students are encouraged to think creatively about the problem from different angles, e.g., by standing in the shoes of climate skeptics – evaluating their “data”, how they present them, the arguments put forth by them – in order to develop a strategic approach to best communicate the issues to a variety of stakeholders. Project assignments will allow students to focus on one particular environmental issue in other part of the world, attempting to address the issue in the very different geographical, cultural, economic and political contexts from that of the students. Example projects can be “How to use geoengineering to combat climate change?”, “How can Sub-Saharan Africa secure its future food supply under the threat of climate change?”, etc. In the course, students will define and understand the problem. They shall form groups and discuss their own point of views and search information on the evidence of the corresponding arguments.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC3800.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of this course, the students will be able to

1. gain understanding of basic climate science behind the global warming issue, including how Earth’s global temperature has been measured by scientists and how the global average temperature is calculated
2. study observations from different components of Earth besides the atmosphere, such as ocean and cryosphere observations, and how these components are affected by the human activities
3. study how the scientific problem is presented and perceived in the society and learn how to use one’s scientific knowledge to identify possible solutions

The active incorporation of elements of innovation and design in the course is critical in allowing the students to develop a keen scientific mindset and logic flow to understand the complexity of the climate change and energy issues (Learning Outcome #1), and to dissect the reasons behind contradictory arguments in a society (Learning Outcome #3). Critical evaluation of the arguments from both sides (climate alarmists vs. skeptics) will ultimately allow them to develop their own stance in the issue, and think critically about the ways to move the world forward towards sustainability (Learning Outcome #3).

The incorporation of global citizenship and social enterprisingness is also crucial for students to develop a keen awareness of how environmental issues affect people in a huge variety of geographical and cultural contexts (Learning Outcome #2), and think critically about what kinds of solutions best fit a certain region (Learning Outcome #3).
courses.course_syllabus:
* Ch1 Changing Planet - global warming and basic climate science
+ Introduction - what is happening and basic climate science
+ Human-induced climate change - why you should take it seriously
+ Human-induced climate change - arguments offered by those who dissent
+ Air quality and its relation to climate change
+ Introductory and Guided Tour of MoCC + Eco-Tour (Sep 8, 2025)
* Ch2 Energy and Greenhouse Gases
+ Energy basics
+ The contemporary US energy system overview, including a comparison with China
+ Coal (abundant but problematic)
+ Oil (a vilatile past, an uncertain future)
+ Natural gas (the least polluting of the fossil fuels)
+ Nuclear power (an optimistic beginning, a clouded future)
+ Power from wind (opportunities and challenges)
+ Power from the sun (abundant but expensive)
+ Hydro: power from running water
+ Earth heat and lunar gravity geothermal and tidal energy
+ Plant biomass as a substitute for oil in transportation
* Ch3 Environment and Climate Policies
+ Limiting US and Chinese Emissions: the Beijing Agreement
+ Vision for a low-carbon-energy future
+ Clean air actions
* Ch4 An AI digital world and its role in global environmental change
+ What is AI, and how does it affect different aspects of our lives?
+ The role of AI in *Global Environmental Change*
+ AI ethics in assisting research

courses.description:
This course aims to provide a comprehensive foundation and holistic view on corporate sustainability and the essential skills needed to become a Certified ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) Planner. Students will explore various aspects of sustainability, ESG practices, CEPAR approach and their integration into corporate strategies for long-term value creation.

Students who have successfully completed this course are eligible to apply for membership of International Chamber of Sustainable Development (ICSD) and gain the authorization to use the trademark designation: Certified ESG Planner CEP® (optional and annual fee applies)

Details: https://www.icsd-global.org/certified-esg-planner
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Define the principles and practices of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), corporate sustainability and ESG.
* Describe the history, benefits, and mandatory requirements of ESG reporting.
* Relate green finance and Fintech to corporate sustainability with consideration on corporate strategy and competitiveness.
* Discuss the CEPAR five-step approach and its application in ESG integration investment analysis approach.
* Evaluate and analyze material ESG and sustainability issues among companies.

Module 1: Corporate Sustainability Foundation (Sessions 1 to 2)

2. Knowledge of the classification and physical properties of soil.
3. A basic understanding of the mechanics of soil, soil consolidation and shear failure.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Petrology
courses.description: Petrology is an essential subfield of Earth and Environmental Sciences because the rock record is the foundation for interpreting Earth history and internal processes. Petrologic concepts help us understand the Earth system, with connections to related fields such as geochemistry, geophysics, mineralogy, structural geology and geodynamics. This course broadly surveys the formation, distribution, chemical composition, mineral associations, and internal texture and structures in rocks of the earth’s crust and upper mantle, and establishes it relation to global tectonic environments. Class lectures are supplemented by lab exercise, demonstrations and tutorials (e.g. study of rock hand specimens, understand phase rule, use of optical microscope etc.).
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC4120. Pre-requisite: EESC2010 or ESSC2010.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to gain the following abilities:

1. Gain the skills to identify different rock types (igneous and metamorphic basically).
2. Good knowledge of typical mineral assemblages of different rocks.
3. Use optical polarizing microscope to distinguish: (1) different minerals based on their optical properties, (2) typical igneous and metamorphic micro-textures and structures, (3) featured mineral assemblages corresponding to different P-T conditions.
4. Understand phase equilibrium and phase rule and their applications in understanding the rock forming process.
5. Understand different metamorphic facies and their significance of variable tectonic settings.
6. Use petrologic concepts and theories to address topics of societal interests, including natural hazards, natural resources, and human health issues.

The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.
courses.course_syllabus:
* Introduction and practice of Electronic Microscope for Petrologic Study
* Phase Rule
* Igneous Petrology
* Sedimentary Petrology
* Metamorphic Petrology

courses.title: Seismology
courses.description: This course focuses on the concepts and theory of elastic wave propagation and physics of earthquake. The topics covered include the Earth’s internal structure, material elasticity, travel time tomography, seismic ray theory, ray tracing, and earthquake source parameter. Methods and field practice of seismological survey will also be introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC4140. Pre-requisite: (EESC3120 or ESSC3120) or ESSC3010.
courses.learning_outcomes: At the completion of this course, the students will be able to know about Earth’s inner structure, understand principles of elastic wave propagation and earthquake physics, seismic imaging, as well as seismic exploration. The students will also be trained with extensive computing exercises. The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction

* Overview of seismology
* Scalar wave equation and solutions
* Wave reflection and transmission; propagating and standing waves

Stress and Strain

* Stress tensor
* Eigenvalue and eigenvectors
* Equation of motion
* Strain and rotation
* Elasticity and constitutive law

Wave propagation

* Plane waves
* Geometrical spreading
* Wave propagation in layered medium
* Body waves
* Post-critical reflection and surface waves
* Rayleigh and Love waves

Earthquake source kinematics

* Moment tensor solution
* Isotropic and CLVD components
* Stress drop

Earthquake rupture dynamics

* Friction law
* Coseismic process and earthquake cycles

* Compulsory field trip (subject to available financial support):

Xinfengjiang water reservoir (understanding basics and operation of earthquake engineering for a large facility, specified designs to reduce earthquake shaking, deploying seismic instruments; 8 hours)

courses.description: What is the mechanism of plate tectonics and a variety of geological phenomena, such as seafloor spreading, mountain building, volcanoes, earthquakes, faulting? Geodynamics is going to answer those questions through observations and models of the large-scale behaviors of the solid Earth. The goal of the course is to develop a thorough understanding of physical processes, such as lithospheric deformation, heat transfer, and mantle flow. Lectures, paper discussions, and tutorials will help you to understand the principle of geodynamics, learn how to develop simple but useful models, and explain how the Earth evolves.

courses.title: Land-Atmosphere Interactions and Boundary-Layer Meteorology
courses.description: This course introduces the physical, chemical and biological processes governing the exchange of energy, momentum, water and other chemical materials between the atmosphere and land surface, including a formal introduction into boundary-layer meteorology. Topics covered include the basic equations and concepts of heat, momentum and mass conservation and transfer at the land-atmosphere interface; soil physics and hydrometeorology; temperature, humidity and wind distribution in the atmospheric boundary layer; theories and observations of turbulence; boundary layer dynamics; biometeorology and ecophysiology of land plants; canopy and ecosystem exchange; and land use change and urbanization. Applications to weather phenomena, air pollution, forestry, agriculture and ecosystem management will be emphasized throughout.

(Course Outcomes as related to Programme Learning Outcomes) -- The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Chemistry of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organic Matter
courses.description:
The course "Chemistry of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organic Matter" covers the study of the chemical composition, properties, and reactivity of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments. Students will learn about the sources, transformation, and fate of organic matter in natural systems, as well as the impact of human activities on organic matter cycling. Topics may include the structure and function of organic molecules, biogeochemical processes involving organic matter, analytical techniques for studying organic matter, and the role of organic matter in ecosystem functioning. This course may also explore the implications of organic matter chemistry for environmental quality, carbon cycling, and nutrient dynamics in natural systems.

Advisory: 2 years college course works in Chemistry, Physics, and Mathematics is recommended.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students are expected to:

1. Understand the chemical composition and properties of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments.
2. Describe the sources, transformation, and fate of organic matter in natural systems.
3. Analyse the impact of human activities on organic matter cycling in ecosystems.
4. Apply analytical techniques to study organic matter in environmental samples.
5. Evaluate the role of organic matter in ecosystem functioning and environmental quality.
6. Discuss the implications of organic matter chemistry for carbon cycling and nutrient dynamics in natural systems.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to Organic Matter Chemistry

* Definition and characteristics of organic matter
* Sources of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments

Week 2: Structure and Function of Organic Molecules

* Molecular composition and properties of organic matter
* Functional groups and reactivity of organic molecules

Week 3: Biogeochemical Processes Involving Organic Matter

* Decomposition and mineralization of organic matter
* Microbial transformations and nutrient cycling

Week 4: Analytical Techniques for Studying Organic Matter

* Spectroscopic methods (FTIR, NMR, UV-Vis)
* Chromatographic techniques (GC-MS, HPLC)

Week 5: Human Impacts on Organic Matter Cycling

* Pollution and contamination of organic matter
* Effects of land use change and climate change on organic matter dynamics

Week 6: Role of Organic Matter in Ecosystem Functioning

* Carbon sequestration and storage
* Nutrient retention and cycling in ecosystems

Week 7: Environmental Quality and Organic Matter Chemistry

* Organic matter as a source of contaminants
* Implications for water quality and ecosystem health

Week 8: Biogeochemical Cycling of Organic Matter in Aquatic Systems

* Organic matter dynamics in lakes, rivers, and oceans
* Impact of water chemistry on organic matter transformations

Week 9: Biogeochemical Cycling of Organic Matter in Terrestrial Systems

* Organic matter cycling in soils and terrestrial ecosystems
* Influence of vegetation and climate on organic matter dynamics

Week 10: Organic Matter and Climate Change

* Role of organic matter in carbon sequestration and greenhouse gas emissions
* Feedback mechanisms between organic matter cycling and climate

Week 11: Case Studies in Organic Matter Chemistry

* Application of organic matter chemistry principles to real-world environmental issues
* Case studies on organic matter in pollution remediation and ecosystem restoration

Week 12: Research Methods in Organic Matter Chemistry

* Designing and conducting experiments related to organic matter analysis
* Data interpretation and presentation of research findings

Week 13: Future Directions in Organic Matter Research

* Emerging trends and challenges in the study of organic matter
* Opportunities for further research and applications in organic matter chemistry

courses.description: This course presents the recent understanding and advancements in global biogeochemistry. The lectures are on a range of topics, covering major elemental cycles from the early Earth to future projections and from the lithosphere to the atmosphere, with an emphasis on the Ocean. Advanced concepts of application of biogeochemistry will further be discussed with an emphasis on environmental pollution and global climate change. The fundamentals of major elemental cycles like carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, oxygen, sulphur, and silicate will also be covered.
courses.enrollment_requirement:

2) Not for those taken ENSC/EESC3330. 
3) Need basic knowledge in environmental science and chemistry.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students are expected to:

1. Understand the key processes regulating elemental cycling in the environment, focusing on oceans.
2. Apply biogeochemistry concepts to global issues like pollution and climate change.
3. Evaluate research on major elemental cycles, including carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, oxygen, sulfur, and silicate.

courses.description:
This course covers the theoretical basis and practical applications of data analysis relevant for earth and environmental sciences. This course aims to introduce students to Earth and environmental data manipulation, from sampling, reading and writing, to statistical analysis and parameter estimation, to time series analysis, to plotting and visualization. Topics include: digital signal processing, sampling techniques; probability distributions; hypothesis testing; correlation analysis; linear and nonlinear regression; statistical forecasting, harmonic analysis and spectral analysis; principle component analysis; and geostatistics. Tutorials will be based on real geophysical examples including remote sensing and in situ observations, as well as model data.

Previous elementary coding experience in Python 3 is recommended.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC4510. Pre-requisite: (STAT1011 or 1012), and ((EESC2030 or ESSC2030) or PHYS2061).
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will become knowledgeable about the acquisition, pre-processing and analysis of geophysical and environmental data, including remote sensing, in situ observation and model data. Students will acquire knowledge on techniques for data processing, including visualization methods and statistical analysis, and on the applications of these data and tools to geophysical and environmental problems. The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principles, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students can also acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.
courses.course_syllabus:
* Introduction; Exploratory Data Analysis; Correlation Analysis
* Parametric Probability Distribution : Discrete
* Parametric Probability Distribution : Continuous
* Hypothesis Testing (Parametric Distributions)
* Nonparametric Tests and Resampling Methods
* Statistical Forecasting: Regression
* Predictor Selection & Principal Component Analysis
* Time Series Analysis
* Fourier Transform
* Cluster Analysis
* Machine Learning

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Remote Sensing – Principles and Applications
courses.description: Remote sensing observations are critical for monitoring regional and global changes, determining spatial and temporal variability of the Earth System, and addressing fundamental global issues. This course introduces the basic physical principles of remote sensing, including electromagnetic waves and radiation, optical, microwave, and non-imaging remote sensing. It also presents key concepts and examples on remote sensing applications in Earth system science (such as atmosphere, lithosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere) and global changes. This course also provides computer-based lab exercises that facilitate the understanding of remote sensing principles and the processing of remote sensing data.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESSC4540. Pre-requisite: (EESC2010 or ESSC2010) &(EESC2020 or ESSC2020) & (MATH1010 or 1030 or 1510 or 1520) & (PHYS1001 or 1002 or 1111 or 1113) & ((EESC2030 or ESSC2030) or PHYS2061), or permission of instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Practical Atmospheric Modeling
courses.description: This course introduces the basic approach in working with LINUX and fundamental principles of atmospheric modeling and provides hands-on practice to perform numerical simulations on University computing cluster. It begins with the concept of model and reviewing of physical and chemical processes in the atmosphere. After that, this course will cover introduction and hands-on practice of the three basic processes in atmospheric simulation: pre-processing data, modeling, and post-processing data. The pre-processing process refers to the preparation of data from various sources by different techniques such as the generation of emission inventories for atmospheric chemistry modeling. It then introduces numerical modeling basics such as “model equations and numerical approaches”. For post-processing, this course offers different skills in visualization of the model output and evaluation of model output against real-world observations. This course also introduces advanced knowledge in “model coupling” and “inverse modeling”. Finally, this course provides hands-on practice on simple model development experiments. During the course, we will cover basic Linux environment commands to perform the main tasks mentioned above. Overall, students can anticipate grasping basic modeling concepts and skills from this course, which are of great value to their future careers in both academia and industry.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: (EESC2020 or ESSC2020) and (MATH2550 or EESC2520 or PHYS2051) and (PHYS1001 or 1002 or 1111 or 1113), or permission of instructor.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Able to explain the major chemical and physical processes that affect the spatiotemporal variations of major atmospheric composition.
2. Explain the basic framework of atmospheric chemistry models including their input and output.
3. Understand what an emission inventory is and be able to pre-process emission inventory for atmospheric chemistry models.
4. Able to compile and run an atmospheric chemistry model.
5. Capable of visualizing model outputs and evaluating model outputs against observations.
6. Have the skills to sort out model structure by reading model codes and then revise model codes according to research needs.
courses.course_syllabus:
* The concept of model
* Processes in the atmosphere I - physical processes
* Processes in the atmosphere II - chemical processes
* Surface fluxes I - emission
* Surface fluxes II - deposition
* Model equations and numerical approaches
* Formulations of radiative, chemical, and aerosol rates
* Parameterization of subgrid-scale processes
* Post-processing model output data
* Model evaluation
* Model coupling
* Inverse modeling
* Model development experiments

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Scientific Applications in Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Analysis
courses.description:
This course provides a holistic overview of corporate sustainability, and the application of scientific data and tools in Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) analysis. The course introduces financing sustainability initiatives, exploring how ESG factors influence investment risks, returns, and corporate performance.

The curriculum introduces fundamental concepts in the climate, ecological and sustainability sciences, providing the context to understand key concepts such as global megatrends and ESG value drivers in shaping the industry landscape.

Students will learn to analyze various ESG investment approaches and strategies, as well as how to embed these criteria within the investment process and valuation models. The course also provides an introduction to sustainable investing across diverse asset classes, key ESG reporting frameworks, and the importance of public accountability.

Upon completion, this course will prepare students to sit for the "Certified ESG Analyst (CESGA)" professional certification offered by EFFAS (The European Federation of Financial Analysts Societies).
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students should be able to:

1. Understand how core scientific principles provide the basis for assessing environmental and social risks and opportunities
2. Understand the body of scientific evidence underpinning the UN SDGs to recognize their role as an essential framework for mitigating systemic global risks
3. Explain how global megatrends and ESG value drivers influence investment risk, return, and the broader industry landscape
4. Learn to integrate ESG considerations into investment analysis, portfolio management, and valuation models
5. Analyze how ESG factors impact corporate financial performance, business strategy, and long-term value creation
6. Differentiate between key ESG investment strategies (e.g., screening, integration, impact) and their application across diverse asset classes
7. Demonstrate an understanding of key ESG reporting frameworks, including the sources, uses, and limitations of the data
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Scientific Foundations of Environmental & Social Analysis**

* Examine the foundational principles from climate, ecological and sustainability sciences that enable the assessment of environmental and social impacts of human activities

* *The Scientific Imperative of Planetary Boundaries and SDGs**

* Analyze evidence from the environmental and health sciences to establish the critical urgency of the UN SDG framework for securing long-term human prosperity in the context of planetary boundaries

* *Foundations of Sustainable Investment**

* The evolving landscape of sustainable investment, core principles and definitions, ESG value drivers and market demand shaping global investment trends

* *Global and Regional ESG Regulatory Frameworks**

* An overview of the evolving global ESG regulatory landscape, with a deep dive into key frameworks in the European Union and Asia-Pacific region

* *Introduction to ESG Reporting and Materiality**

* Understanding of voluntary vs. mandatory regimes and the convergence of major ESG reporting frameworks and the concept of double materiality

* *ESG Data: Availability, Quality and Usage**

* Evaluation of primary ESG information sources, addressing challenges in raw data availability and quality, and the effective use of indicators

* *Recent Development of Global ESG investing**

* Market drivers and barriers to ESG integration and empirical evidence on ESG's effect on financial performance

* *Sustainable Investing and Financing Across Asset Classes**

* Introduction to the distinctive features of various sustainable investment strategies
* Application of responsible investing principles and tailored ESG strategies across different asset classes

* *ESG Integration in Fundamental Research & Valuation**

* Identifying material ESG factors for corporations and sovereigns through qualitative analysis of governance and sector-specific E&S factors, and translating these findings into quantitative valuation models through adjustments to financial forecasts

* *ESG Integration in Investment Process**

* Understanding the investment process chain and how ESG considerations influence its key stages, from setting investment guidelines and objectives to macro research, asset allocation, and informing the final investment decision

* *ESG Integration in Portfolio Management & Active Ownership**

* Application in portfolio construction, monitoring and management, active ownership strategies (proxy voting and engagement), and developing a robust framework for ESG-integrated due diligence

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Climate Risk Analysis for Sustainable Earth and Environment
courses.description:
This course provides a holistic overview of the science of climate change, its economic consequences, and its impact on financial institutions and markets. It introduces the assessment of climate-related risks and opportunities for financial decision-making.
Students will learn to analyze physical and transition climate risks, as well as their financial impacts on assets, portfolios and business performance. The curriculum also provides an introduction to the integration of climate considerations into business strategies, the transition to Net Zero, and the development of climate-resilient adaptation plans.

Upon completion, this course will prepare students to sit for the “EFFAS Certified Climate Risk Analyst (ECRA)” examination offered by EFFAS (The European Federation of Financial Analysts Societies).
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students should be able to:

1. Understand the science behind climate change and its financial impacts
2. Learn global climate regulations and their effects on financial institutions
3. Identify and model climate-related risks, including asset vulnerability and exposure
4. Quantify the financial impacts of climate-related risks on portfolios and businesses
5. Explore opportunities in sustainable investments and renewable energy
6. Understand climate adaptation plans and how they incorporate 'Net Zero' goals and financial strategies
7. Gain insights into green finance and the growth of sustainable markets
courses.course_syllabus:
* *The Science of Climate Change**

* Delve into the science basis of climate change, its global impacts, and the foundational concepts of mitigation and adaptation, drawing from key IPCC findings

* *Climate Policy, Regulation, and Reporting Standards**

* Examine the formation of global climate policy under the UNFCCC and Paris Agreement, and analyze its subsequent implementation through regional/national legislation (e.g., EU Green Deal) and emerging corporate disclosure standards

* *Identifying and Modeling Climate Risk**

* Covering climate scenarios as well as the key techniques for identifying, modeling, and analyzing the impacts of physical and transition risks

* *Quantifying the Financial Impact of Climate Risk**

* Translate identified climate risks into financial terms by quantifying their impact, and understand the methods for reporting these risks and associated uncertainties in corporate financial statements

* *Climate-Related Opportunities**

* Explore the financial opportunities arising from the climate transition, from new technologies and business models to the value created by organizations that successfully seize climate-related initiatives

* *Corporate Climate Strategy: Net Zero and Adaptation**

* Analyze the components of effective corporate climate strategies, including the development of credible Net Zero roadmaps, carbon accounting, and robust plans for climate adaptation and risk transfer

* *Economic Effects Financial Stability**

* Assess the broader economic consequences of climate change, including its potential effects on economic indicators, and its implications for systemic risk and overall financial stability

* *Instruments of Sustainable and Climate Finance**

* Explore sustainable finance, including green, social, and sustainability-linked finance. Emphasize the importance and impact of sustainable financial practices in supporting climate mitigation and adaptation

* *Climate Risk Across Asset Classes**

* Apply climate risk analysis to specific asset classes, including equity, debt, private equity, and real estate, and learn techniques for portfolio decarbonization and measuring financed GHG emissions

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Petrology
courses.description: Petrology is an essential subfield of Earth System Science because the rock record is the foundation for interpreting Earth history and internal processes. Petrologic concepts help us understand the Earth system, with connections to related fields such as geochemistry, geophysics, mineralogy, structural geology and geodynamics. This course broadly surveys the formation, distribution, chemical composition, mineral associations, and internal texture and structures in rocks of the earth’s crust and upper mantle, and establishes it relation to global tectonic environments. Class lectures are supplemented by lab exercise, demonstrations and tutorials (e.g. study of rock hand specimens, understand phase rule, use of optical microscope etc.).
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Earth and Environmental Sciences;Exclusion: EASC5120.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to gain the following abilities:

1. Gain the skills to identify different rock types (igneous and metamorphic basically).
2. Good knowledge of typical mineral assemblages of different rocks.
3. Use optical polarizing microscope to distinguish: (1) different minerals based on their optical properties, (2) typical igneous and metamorphic micro-textures and structures, (3) featured mineral assemblages corresponding to different P-T conditions.
4. Understand phase equilibrium and phase rule and their applications in understanding the rock forming process.
5. Understand different metamorphic facies and their significance of variable tectonic settings.
6. Use petrologic concepts and theories to address topics of societal interests, including natural hazards, natural resources, and human health issues.

The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.
courses.course_syllabus:
* Introduction and practice of Electronic Microscope for Petrologic Study
* Phase Rule
* Igneous Petrology
* Sedimentary Petrology
* Metamorphic Petrology

courses.title: Land-Atmosphere Interactions and Boundary-Layer Meteorology
courses.description: This course introduces the physical, chemical and biological processes governing the exchange of energy, momentum, water and other chemical materials between the atmosphere and land surface, including a formal introduction into boundary-layer meteorology. Topics covered include the basic equations and concepts of heat, momentum and mass conservation and transfer at the land-atmosphere interface; soil physics and hydrometeorology; temperature, humidity and wind distribution in the atmospheric boundary layer; theories and observations of turbulence; stability and boundary-layer dynamics; biometeorology and ecophysiology of land plants; canopy and ecosystem exchange; and land use change and urbanization. Applications to weather phenomena, air pollution, forestry, agriculture and ecosystem management will be emphasized throughout.

courses.learning_outcomes: At the completion of this course, the students will be able to 1) Gain knowledge of physical, chemical and biological processes of land-atmosphere interactions and boundary layer dynamics; and 2) Develop the skill and ability to understand weather and climate variability related to land-atmosphere interactions. (Course Outcomes as related to Programme Learning Outcomes) -- The students will develop: 1) the ability to evaluate an issue critically based on evidence and scientific principle, 2) to acquire knowledge effectively by self-study and using information technology, and to work independently, 3) to work effectively in a team, and 4) to present information in a clear, concise and logical manner. The students also can acquire a responsible attitude in study and work, and have motivation for life-long learning.
