courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Biogeochemistry of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organic Matter
courses.description:
The course "Biogeochemistry of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organic Matter" covers the study of the chemical composition, properties, and reactivity of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments. Students will learn about the sources, transformation, and fate of organic matter in natural systems, as well as the impact of human activities on organic matter cycling. Topics may include the structure and function of organic molecules, biogeochemical processes involving organic matter, analytical techniques for studying organic matter, and the role of organic matter in ecosystem functioning. This course may also explore the implications of organic matter biogeochemistry for environmental quality, carbon cycling, and nutrient dynamics in natural systems.

Advisory: 2 years college course works in Chemistry, Physics, and Mathematics is recommended.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students in MPhil-PhD Earth and Atmospheric Sciences or MPhil-PhD Earth and Environmental Sciences or MSc in Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability
Exclusion(s): EESC4310
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students are expected to:

1. Understand the chemical composition and properties of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments.
2. Describe the sources, transformation, and fate of organic matter in natural systems.
3. Analyse the impact of human activities on organic matter cycling in ecosystems.
4. Apply analytical techniques to study organic matter in environmental samples.
5. Evaluate the role of organic matter in ecosystem functioning and environmental quality.
6. Discuss the implications of organic matter chemistry for carbon cycling and nutrient dynamics in natural systems.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to Organic Matter Chemistry

* Definition and characteristics of organic matter
* Sources of organic matter in aquatic and terrestrial environments

Week 2: Structure and Function of Organic Molecules

* Molecular composition and properties of organic matter
* Functional groups and reactivity of organic molecules

Week 3: Biogeochemical Processes Involving Organic Matter

* Decomposition and mineralization of organic matter
* Microbial transformations and nutrient cycling

Week 4: Analytical Techniques for Studying Organic Matter

* Spectroscopic methods (FTIR, NMR, UV-Vis)
* Chromatographic techniques (GC-MS, HPLC)

Week 5: Human Impacts on Organic Matter Cycling

* Pollution and contamination of organic matter
* Effects of land use change and climate change on organic matter dynamics

Week 6: Role of Organic Matter in Ecosystem Functioning

* Carbon sequestration and storage
* Nutrient retention and cycling in ecosystems

Week 7: Environmental Quality and Organic Matter Chemistry

* Organic matter as a source of contaminants
* Implications for water quality and ecosystem health

Week 8: Biogeochemical Cycling of Organic Matter in Aquatic Systems

* Organic matter dynamics in lakes, rivers, and oceans
* Impact of water chemistry on organic matter transformations

Week 9: Biogeochemical Cycling of Organic Matter in Terrestrial Systems

* Organic matter cycling in soils and terrestrial ecosystems
* Influence of vegetation and climate on organic matter dynamics

Week 10: Organic Matter and Climate Change

* Role of organic matter in carbon sequestration and greenhouse gas emissions
* Feedback mechanisms between organic matter cycling and climate

Week 11: Case Studies in Organic Matter Chemistry

* Application of organic matter chemistry principles to real-world environmental issues
* Case studies on organic matter in pollution remediation and ecosystem restoration

Week 12: Research Methods in Organic Matter Chemistry

* Designing and conducting experiments related to organic matter analysis
* Data interpretation and presentation of research findings

Week 13: Future Directions in Organic Matter Research

* Emerging trends and challenges in the study of organic matter
* Opportunities for further research and applications in organic matter chemistry

courses.description:
This course covers the theoretical basis and practical applications of data analysis relevant for earth system science. This course aims to introduce students to Earth and environmental data manipulation, from sampling, reading and writing, to statistical analysis and parameter estimation, to time series analysis, to plotting and visualization. Topics include: digital signal processing, sampling techniques; probability distributions; hypothesis testing; correlation analysis; linear and nonlinear regression; statistical forecasting, harmonic analysis and spectral analysis; principle component analysis; and geostatistics. Tutorials will be based on real geophysical examples including remote sensing and in situ observations, as well as model data.

Previous elementary coding experience in Python 3 is recommended.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Practical Atmospheric Modeling
courses.description: This course introduces the basic approach in working with LINUX and fundamental principles of atmospheric modeling and provides hands-on practice to perform numerical simulations on University computing cluster. It begins with the concept of model and reviewing of physical and chemical processes in the atmosphere. After that, this course will cover introduction and hands-on practice of the three basic processes in atmospheric simulation: pre-processing data, modeling, and post-processing data. The pre-processing process refers to the preparation of data from various sources by different techniques such as the generation of emission inventories for atmospheric chemistry modeling. It then introduces numerical modeling basics such as “model equations and numerical approaches”. For post-processing, this course offers different skills in visualization of the model output and evaluation of model output against real-world observations. This course also introduces advanced knowledge in “model coupling” and “inverse modeling”. Finally, this course provides hands-on practice on simple model development experiments. During the course, we will cover basic Linux environment commands to perform the main tasks mentioned above. Overall, students can anticipate grasping basic modeling concepts and skills from this course, which are of great value to their future careers in both academia and industry.

1. Able to explain the major chemical and physical processes that affect spatiotemporal variations of major atmospheric composition.
2. Explain the basic framework of atmospheric chemistry models including their input and output.
3. Understand what an emission inventory is and be able to pre-process emission inventory for atmospheric chemistry models.
4. Able to compile and run an atmospheric chemistry model.
5. Capable of visualizing model outputs and evaluating model outputs against observations.
6. Have the skills to sort out model structure by reading model codes and then revise model codes according to research needs.
courses.course_syllabus:
* The concept of model
* Processes in the atmosphere I - physical processes
* Processes in the atmosphere II - chemical processes
* Surface fluxes I - emission
* Surface fluxes II - deposition
* Model equations and numerical approaches
* Formulations of radiative, chemical, and aerosol rates
* Parameterization of subgrid-scale processes
* Post-processing model output data
* Model evaluation
* Model coupling
* Inverse modeling
* Model development experiments

Students will explore advanced concepts in solid earth systems while discovering how modern analytical methods are revolutionizing our understanding of Earth processes.

The course covers frontier topics in seismology, structural geology, plate tectonics, and natural hazards, incorporating innovative techniques in geophysical monitoring and earth observation. Through a combination of theoretical foundations and real-world applications, students will examine how traditional geological and geophysical methods are enhanced by emerging technologies, advanced data analysis, and modern sensor systems. Special emphasis will be placed on understanding complex Earth processes and their interactions with environmental systems across different spatial and temporal scales.

courses.title: Systems Thinking and Diagnostics for Sustainable Earth and Environment
courses.description:
This course introduces students to systems thinking approaches and diagnostic methods for analyzing complex environmental challenges. Students will learn to understand and analyze environmental systems as interconnected networks of physical, biological, and human components, developing skills to diagnose environmental problems through a holistic lens.

The course emphasizes the identification and analysis of feedback loops, emergent properties, and cross-scale interactions in environmental systems. Students will learn to apply diagnostic frameworks to decompose complex environmental problems, identify key system drivers, and understand cascade effects across different spatial and temporal scales. Through case studies of real-world environmental challenges, students will develop proficiency in systems mapping, causal loop analysis, and diagnostic approaches to environmental problem-solving.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Explain the fundamental principles of systems thinking and articulate how physical, biological, and human components interact within environmental systems, using appropriate scientific terminology and concepts.
• Analyze complex environmental challenges by decomposing them into interconnected components, identifying feedback loops, and evaluating cross-scale interactions using systematic diagnostic frameworks.
• Design comprehensive system maps for real-world environmental problems, evaluate the effectiveness of different diagnostic approaches, and develop evidence-based recommendations that account for cascade effects across spatial and temporal scales.

• Early warning signals

Human-Environment Interactions

• Urban sustainability challenges
Uncertainty and Complexity

• Uncertainty analysis methods
Applied Systems Analysis
• Problem-solving workshops
• Real-world case studies
• Group project work
Integration and Synthesis
• Final project presentations
• Systems thinking in practice
• Future challenges and opportunities

courses.description:
This specialized course offers students the opportunity to conduct an in-depth literature review and analysis of a specific contemporary topic in earth and environmental data analytics under faculty supervision. Students will work closely with a professor to explore cutting-edge developments and emerging trends in their chosen area of interest within the field.

The course is structured around independent research and short lecture modules. Students will learn to critically evaluate scientific literature, synthesize complex information, and develop comprehensive understanding of their chosen topic. Through this guided exploration, students will gain expertise in a specialized area while developing essential skills in scientific literature review, academic writing, and research methodology.

The final outcome will be an oral presentation that demonstrates thorough understanding of the chosen topic's current state of knowledge, methodological approaches, and future research directions. This course provides valuable experience in academic research and scientific writing, preparing students for potential research careers or advanced studies in earth and environmental sciences.

• Develop and present evidence-based arguments about the state of research in the chosen topic, critically assess the strengths and limitations of current methodologies, and propose directions for future research based on identified knowledge gaps.
• Present a well-structured, scholarly oral presentation that critically evaluates current research, effectively uses visual aids, and engages in academic discourse about the implications and future directions of the chosen topic, demonstrating mastery of both content and professional communication skills.

courses.subject: EESC
courses.title: Scientific Applications in Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Analysis
courses.description:
This course aims to provide a comprehensive foundation and holistic view on corporate sustainability, and the utilization of scientific data and tools in Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) analysis. The course also provides an overview of financing sustainability initiatives, exploring how ESG factors influence investment risks, returns, and corporate performance.

The curriculum first covers fundamental concepts in the climate, ecological and sustainability sciences, providing the context to introduce key concepts such as global megatrends and ESG value drivers in shaping the industry landscape. Students will examine various ESG investment approaches and strategies, developing a practical understanding of how to embed these criteria within the investment process and valuation models. The course also covers sustainable investing across diverse asset classes, key ESG reporting frameworks, and the importance of public accountability.

By integrating these topics, the curriculum equips students with comprehensive understanding of the field. Upon completion, it prepares students to sit for the “Certified ESG Analyst (CESGA)” professional certification offered by EFFAS (The European Federation of Financial Analysts Societies).
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability; or in MPhil-PhD Earth and Environmental Sciences
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students should be able to:
1. Evaluate how core scientific principles provide the basis for assessing environmental and social risks and opportunities.
2. Assess the body of scientific evidence underpinning the UN SDGs to defend their role as an essential framework for mitigating systemic global risks and ensuring human prosperity.
3. Explain how global megatrends and ESG value drivers influence investment risk, return, and the broader industry landscape
4. Integrate ESG considerations into investment analysis, portfolio management, and valuation models
5. Analyze how ESG factors impact corporate financial performance, business strategy, and long-term value creation
6. Differentiate between key ESG investment strategies (e.g., screening, integration, impact) and their application across diverse asset classes
7. Demonstrate an understanding of key ESG reporting frameworks, including the sources, uses, and limitations of the data
courses.course_syllabus:
Scientific Foundations of Environmental & Social Analysis
• Examine the foundational principles from climate, ecological and sustainability sciences that enable the assessment of environmental and social impacts of human activities.

The Scientific Imperative of Planetary Boundaries and SDGs
• Analyze evidence from the environmental and health sciences to establish the critical urgency of the UN SDG framework for securing long-term human prosperity in the context of planetary boundaries.

Foundations of Sustainable Investment
• The evolving landscape of sustainable investment, core principles and definitions, ESG value drivers and market demand shaping global investment trends

Global and Regional ESG Regulatory Frameworks
• An overview of the evolving global ESG regulatory landscape, with a deep dive into key frameworks in the European Union and Asia-Pacific region.

Introduction to ESG Reporting and Materiality
• Understanding of voluntary vs. mandatory regimes and the convergence of major ESG reporting frameworks and the concept of double materiality

ESG Data: Availability, Quality and Usage
• Evaluation of primary ESG information sources, addressing challenges in raw data availability and quality, and the effective use of indicators

Recent Development of Global ESG investing
• Market drivers and barriers to ESG integration and empirical evidence on ESG's effect on financial performance

• Sustainable Investing and Financing Across Asset Classes

Introduction to the distinctive features of various sustainable investment strategies
• Application of responsible investing principles and tailored ESG strategies across different asset classes

ESG Integration in Fundamental Research & Valuation
• Identifying material ESG factors for corporations and sovereigns through qualitative analysis of governance and sector-specific E&S factors, and translating these findings into quantitative valuation models through adjustments to financial forecasts

ESG Integration in Investment Process
• Understanding the investment process chain and how ESG considerations influence its key stages, from setting investment guidelines and objectives to macro research, asset allocation, and informing the final investment decision

ESG Integration in Portfolio Management & Active Ownership
• Application in portfolio construction, monitoring and management, active ownership strategies (proxy voting and engagement), and developing a robust framework for ESG-integrated due diligence

courses.description:
This capstone research course builds upon the foundation established in Special Topics, allowing students to conduct original research on their previously reviewed topic. Under faculty supervision, students will transform their comprehensive literature review into an empirical research project, following scientific methodology to produce a manuscript in research article format.

Students will design and execute a focused research project, applying appropriate analytical methods and tools to address specific research questions in their chosen area. The course emphasizes the complete research process, from hypothesis formulation and study design to data analysis, interpretation, and scientific writing. Regular consultations with the supervising professor will guide students through the research process and manuscript preparation.

The final deliverable will be a research paper formatted according to scientific journal standards, potentially suitable for publication submission. The paper will include original analysis, results, and discussion of findings in the context of existing literature. Students will also be required to present their research findings in a seminar format, developing their scientific communication skills.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Pre-requisite: EESC5750 or permission of instructor
For students in MSc Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Transform theoretical knowledge into empirical research by designing a methodologically sound study, formulating testable hypotheses, and selecting appropriate analytical approaches, demonstrating ability to bridge theoretical understanding with practical research implementation.
• Apply advanced data analysis techniques and tools to conduct original research, showing mastery in handling environmental data, implementing appropriate statistical methods, and producing reliable analytical results.
• Integrate research findings with existing literature to produce a publication-quality manuscript and deliver a professional research presentation, demonstrating comprehensive scientific writing skills and ability to effectively communicate complex research findings to diverse audiences.

Data Collection/Preparation

courses.title: Climate Risk Analysis for Sustainable Earth and Environment
courses.description:
The course explores the science of climate change, its economic consequences, and how it affects financial institutions and markets, and formally introduces the assessment of climate-related risks for financial decision making. Students will learn to identify, model, and analyze physical and transition climate risks, including measuring their financial impacts on assets, portfolios, and business performance. The curriculum also covers the integration of climate considerations into business strategies, the transition to Net Zero, and the development of climate-resilient adaptation plans.

Upon completion, this course will prepare students to sit for the “EFFAS Certified Climate Risk Analyst (ECRA)” examination offered by EFFAS (The European Federation of Financial Analysts Societies).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students in MSc in Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability
Exclusion(s): EESC4780
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students should be able to:
1. Understand the science behind climate change and its financial impacts
2. Learn global climate regulations and their effects on financial institutions
3. Identify and model climate-related risks, including asset vulnerability and exposure
4. Quantify the financial impacts of climate-related risks on portfolios and businesses
5. Explore opportunities in sustainable investments and renewable energy
6. Understand climate adaptation plans and how they incorporate 'Net Zero' goals and financial strategies
7. Gain insights into green finance and the growth of sustainable markets
courses.course_syllabus:
The Science of Climate Change
- Delve into the science basis of climate change, its global impacts, and the foundational concepts of mitigation and adaptation, drawing from key IPCC findings

Climate Policy, Regulation, and Reporting Standards
- Examine the formation of global climate policy under the UNFCCC and Paris Agreement, and analyze its subsequent implementation through regional/national legislation (e.g., EU Green Deal) and emerging corporate disclosure standards

Identifying and Modeling Climate Risk
- Covering climate scenarios as well as the key techniques for identifying, modeling, and analyzing the impacts of physical and transition risks

Quantifying the Financial Impact of Climate Risk
- Translate identified climate risks into financial terms by quantifying their impact, and understand the methods for reporting these risks and associated uncertainties in corporate financial statements

Climate-Related Opportunities
- Explore the financial opportunities arising from the climate transition, from new technologies and business models to the value created by organizations that successfully seize climate-related initiatives

Corporate Climate Strategy: Net Zero and Adaptation
- Analyze the components of effective corporate climate strategies, including the development of credible Net Zero roadmaps, carbon accounting, and robust plans for climate adaptation and risk transfer

Economic Effects Financial Stability
- Assess the broader economic consequences of climate change, including its potential effects on economic indicators, and its implications for systemic risk and overall financial stability

Instruments of Sustainable and Climate Finance
 Explore sustainable finance, including green, social, and sustainability-linked finance. Emphasize the importance and impact of sustainable financial practices in supporting climate mitigation and adaptation

Climate Risk Across Asset Classes
- Apply climate risk analysis to specific asset classes, including equity, debt, private equity, and real estate, and learn techniques for portfolio decarbonization and measuring financed GHG emissions

courses.description:
This optional summer research course provides an extended opportunity for students to further develop their research project from the previous semester. Students who show exceptional progress and potential in their Research Project can continue their investigation, deepen their analysis, and enhance the quality of their research output under faculty supervision.

The course allows students to expand their research scope, incorporate additional data analysis, conduct supplementary experiments, or explore new dimensions of their research topic. This extended research period enables more sophisticated analysis, robust validation of results, and potential development of new methodological approaches. Students will benefit from continued mentorship and access to departmental resources during the summer term.

The final outcome will be an enhanced research manuscript that demonstrates greater depth, sophistication, and scientific rigor. Students may also prepare supplementary materials such as data repositories, analytical codes, or detailed methodological protocols. This extended research experience may lead to submission-ready manuscripts for peer-reviewed journals or form the basis for future doctoral research.

Must have completed EESC 5750 and 5770. Credits not counted toward programme requirements.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Pre-requisite = EESC5750 and EESC5770 or permission of instructor
For students in MSc Earth and Environmental Analytics for Sustainability
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Expand and deepen original research through advanced analytical approaches, additional data integration, or methodological refinements, demonstrating ability to elevate research quality beyond initial project scope.
• Develop and implement sophisticated research components such as validation procedures, sensitivity analyses, or comparative methodologies, showing mastery in advanced environmental data analytics and research methodologies.
• Produce publication-quality research outputs including detailed supplementary materials, documented analytical procedures, and comprehensive data management protocols, demonstrating readiness for professional research dissemination or doctoral-level work.

Comprehensive Results Development
Enhanced Manuscript Development
